name: Hafnium
name_origin: Named after Hafnia, the Latin name for Copenhagen, Denmark, where the element was discovered in 1923 by Dirk Coster and George de Hevesy.

discovery.discoverer: Dirk Coster and George de Hevesy

occurrence.abundance_summary: Hafnium is moderately rare in Earth's crust at about 3 ppm. It is never found free in nature and almost always occurs alongside zirconium in zirconium-bearing minerals such as zircon and baddeleyite, from which it must be separated by complex chemical processes.
states: trace in igneous and metamorphic rocks

compounds.name: Hafnium dioxide (hafnia)
compounds.summary: A high-permittivity dielectric used as a gate insulator in advanced CMOS transistors, replacing SiO2 in nodes below 45 nm.

compounds.name: Hafnium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: A white crystalline solid used as a precursor for atomic layer deposition of HfO2 thin films and as a catalyst.

compounds.name: Hafnium carbide
compounds.summary: An extremely refractory ceramic with one of the highest known melting points (~3900 C), used in rocket nozzle linings.

compounds.name: Hafnium nitride
compounds.summary: A hard, gold-colored ceramic used as a wear-resistant coating on cutting tools.

compounds.name: Hafnium diboride
compounds.summary: An ultra-high-temperature ceramic used in hypersonic vehicle leading edges and atmospheric reentry components.

uses: Control rods in nuclear reactors, especially naval reactors, due to its very high thermal-neutron capture cross section
Gate dielectric (HfO2) in advanced semiconductor transistors
Alloying agent in superalloys for jet engine turbine blades
Plasma cutting electrodes that operate in oxygen-bearing gases
Refractory ceramics for rocket nozzles and hypersonic leading edges
Getter material in vacuum tubes and incandescent lamps
reactivity_summary: Hafnium is a lustrous silvery metal that is highly resistant to corrosion thanks to a tenacious oxide passivation layer; bulk hafnium is essentially inert in air at room temperature. It resists alkalis, water, and most acids except hydrofluoric acid, but finely divided hafnium powder is pyrophoric. At high temperature it reacts directly with oxygen, nitrogen, carbon, boron, sulfur, and silicon. Its chemistry is almost identical to that of zirconium because of the lanthanide contraction.
isotopes_summary: Hafnium has six naturally occurring isotopes: 174Hf (very long-lived alpha emitter, t1/2 ~2x10^15 yr), 176Hf, 177Hf, 178Hf, 179Hf, and 180Hf, with 180Hf the most abundant (~35%). The metastable nuclear isomer 178m2Hf has attracted attention for its extraordinarily high stored energy density. The 176Lu->176Hf decay underpins the lutetium-hafnium geochronology system.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Hafnium
